one_line_takeaway: Combining static structural (GNN-on-DFG) dataset filtering with runtime prompt paraphrasing cuts hardware-Trojan backdoor attack success in LLM-based RTL fine-tuning from ~94-96% to ~33-37% without hurting VerilogEval functional correctness, but residual per-attack vulnerability (e.g., PIC Trojans at 64-72% ASR) shows static filtering alone is insufficient and the defense is not complete.
key_finding: Baseline (unfiltered fine-tuning) ASR is 94% for Qwen2.5-Coder-14B and 96% for CodeLlama-13B. Offline dataset sanitization alone provides only limited mitigation: ASR drops to 91% (Qwen) and 94% (CodeLlama) -- with per-attack breakdown (Tables III/IV) showing UART remains at 100% ASR under tuning-only for both models. Runtime paraphrasing alone (on models still fine-tuned on raw poisoned data) is markedly more effective, lowering overall ASR to 37% (Qwen) and 40% (CodeLlama). The combined SafeTune (sanitized fine-tuning + runtime paraphrasing) achieves the lowest ASR overall: 33% for Qwen2.5-Coder-14B and 37% for CodeLlama-13B, i.e., reductions of about 65% and 61% relative to baseline respectively. Per-attack-family ASR under full SafeTune (Table III/IV) still shows uneven residual vulnerability, e.g., PIC stays at 72% (Qwen) / 64% (CodeLlama) even under the combined defense, while UART, AES, RSA, SRAM drop substantially. VerilogEval functional correctness is essentially unchanged by sanitization: Qwen2.5-Coder-14B Pass@1/5/10 are 37.1%/45.8%/50.6% for both baseline and SafeTune-tuned versions (identical to three significant figures in the paper's own Table I); CodeLlama-13B shows a negligible Pass@1 drop from 32.6% to 32.0% with Pass@5/10 unchanged at 35.8%/39.1%.
core_contribution: SafeTune is a two-stage, cross-modal dataset-sanitization and runtime-defense framework for protecting LLM fine-tuning on RTL (Verilog) code generation against data-poisoning/backdoor (hardware Trojan) attacks. It jointly filters (1) natural-language prompts, via GTE-large sentence embeddings scored by an XGBoost risk regressor, with the safest of five LLM-generated paraphrases selected per prompt, and (2) RTL code, by converting each design to a Data-Flow Graph (DFG) via PyVerilog and classifying it with a two-layer Graph Isomorphism Network (GIN, 128 hidden units, 0.4 dropout) that flags samples exceeding a Trojan-probability threshold tau for removal. A third component, a runtime prompt-paraphrasing agent, rewrites user prompts at inference time to strip trigger-like lexical/syntactic cues before they reach the (already fine-tuned) model. The paper's stated contributions are explicitly: (i) the first framework to jointly filter prompts and RTL to mitigate backdoor poisoning during fine-tuning data preparation, (ii) the embedding+XGBoost semantic prompt selector, (iii) the GNN-based structural Trojan detector operating on ASTs/DFGs, and (iv) an end-to-end evaluation showing SafeTune reduces attack success rate (ASR) while preserving clean-data utility.
threat_model: Adversary poisons a subset of the fine-tuning corpus D = {(p_i, r_i)} (prompt-RTL pairs) with samples where the prompt contains a rare linguistic trigger and the paired RTL embeds a hardware-Trojan-like payload activated when that trigger appears at inference. The attacker cannot alter model architecture, optimizer, hyperparameters, loss, or downstream verification tools -- capability is limited to corpus poisoning. Attack goal: a fine-tuned model that behaves normally on benign prompts but emits malicious RTL when the trigger is present. Defender has full access to the training corpus but explicitly no trusted clean dataset, no verified Trojan-free RTL references, and no dynamic validation (testbenches/simulation) during filtering -- defense must rely solely on static analysis of prompts and RTL. This threat model is stated by the authors as following the assumptions of prior work (RTL-Breaker, ref [13]), not originated in this paper.
limitations: The paper's own analysis and text acknowledge: (1) offline dataset sanitization alone is 'not enough' -- some Trojan families (notably UART) remain nearly 100% effective even after structural/semantic filtering, meaning the GNN/XGBoost filters miss a substantial share of poisoned samples; (2) even the full combined defense leaves some attack families (PIC in particular, and SRAM for CodeLlama) with high residual ASR (64-72%), so SafeTune reduces but does not eliminate backdoor activation; (3) the defender explicitly has no trusted clean reference or dynamic verification available, so detection is static-analysis-only and the paper does not evaluate what an adaptive attacker aware of the GNN/embedding filters (e.g., crafting DFG structures or paraphrase-robust triggers to evade both stages) could achieve; (4) evaluation is confined to five hardware designs (AES, PIC, RSA, UART, SRAM) and two LLM backbones (Qwen2.5-Coder-14B, CodeLlama-13B) with poisoned samples partly synthesized via ChatGPT-5.1/OSS-GPT-120B rather than drawn from real-world poisoned corpora, raising generalization questions the paper does not address. It is a short special-session paper (5 pages) with no ablation isolating the GNN detector from the prompt-risk regressor's individual contribution to ASR reduction, nor any reported false-positive/false-negative rate for the filters on clean data beyond the aggregate VerilogEval score.

datasets_used: RTL++ (authors' prior work, ref [27]) -- source of benign RTL samples
Trust-Hub (ref [28]) -- source of 70 hardware Trojan seed designs
Prior study ref [29] (Unleashing Ghost) -- additional Trojan seed source
Five circuit designs: AES, PIC, RSA, UART, SRAM -- expanded to 2,500 poisoned samples via ChatGPT-5.1
VerilogEval (ref [14]) -- functional/syntactic correctness benchmark for Pass@k evaluation
Custom split: 1,000 samples for classifier training, 1,000 for LLM fine-tuning, 125 Trojan samples for attack evaluation
quantitative_results: Overall ASR baseline: Qwen2.5-Coder-14B 94%, CodeLlama-13B 96% (Table II)
Overall ASR SafeTune (tuning + runtime): Qwen2.5-Coder-14B 33%, CodeLlama-13B 37% (Table II)
Tuning-only ASR: Qwen 91%, CodeLlama 94% (text, Section VI-C)
Runtime-only ASR: Qwen 37%, CodeLlama 40% (text, Section VI-D)
Per-attack ASR under full SafeTune, Qwen2.5-Coder-14B (Table III): UART 20%, PIC 72%, AES 28%, RSA 28%, SRAM 16%
Per-attack ASR under full SafeTune, CodeLlama-13B (Table IV): UART 20%, PIC 64%, AES 36%, RSA 32%, SRAM 32%
VerilogEval Pass@1/5/10, Qwen2.5-Coder-14B: Base 37.1%/45.8%/50.6% vs SafeTune 37.1%/45.8%/50.6% (Table I, unchanged)
VerilogEval Pass@1/5/10, CodeLlama-13B: Base 32.6%/35.8%/39.1% vs SafeTune 32.0%/35.8%/39.1% (Table I, negligible drop only at Pass@1)
baselines_compared: Qwen2.5-Coder-14B-Instruct fine-tuned on raw unfiltered corpus (LoRA)
CodeLlama-13B-Instruct fine-tuned on raw unfiltered corpus (LoRA)
Ablation: dataset sanitization only (no runtime defense)
Ablation: runtime prompt paraphrasing only (model still fine-tuned on raw data)
